content:
# Joshua Chapter 1 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 226 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_001_page_001.jpg`

1. And it was after the death of Moses, the servant of YHWH, that YHWH spoke to Joshua the son of Nun, the minister of Moses, saying:

2. "Moses my servant is dead. Now therefore arise, cross over this Jordan — you and all this people — into the land that I am giving to them, to the Sons of Israel.

3. Every place upon which the sole of your foot shall tread, I have given it to you, just as I spoke to Moses.

4. From the wilderness and this Lebanon as far as the great river, the River Euphrates — all the land of the Hittites — and as far as the Great Sea toward the setting of the sun, shall be your territory.

5. No man shall stand before you all the days of your life. As I was with Moses, so I will be with you. I will not fail you, and I will not forsake you.

6. Be strong and courageous, for you shall cause this people to inherit the land that I swore to their fathers to give to them.

7. Only be strong and very courageous, being careful to do according to all the Torah that Moses my servant commanded you. Do not turn from it to the right or to the left, so that you may act wisely wherever you go.

8. This book of the Torah shall not depart from your mouth, but you shall meditate upon it day and night, so that you may be careful to do according to all that is written in it. For then you shall make your way prosperous, and then you shall act wisely.

9. Have I not commanded you? Be strong and courageous. Do not be terrified and do not be dismayed, for YHWH your God is with you wherever you go."

10. Then Joshua commanded the officers of the people, saying:

11. "Pass through the midst of the camp and command the people, saying: 'Prepare provisions for yourselves, for within three days you are to cross over this Jordan to go in to possess the land that YHWH your God is giving you to possess.'"

12. And to the Reubenites, and to the Gadites, and to the half-tribe of Manasseh, Joshua spoke, saying:

13. "Remember the word that Moses the servant of YHWH commanded you, saying: 'YHWH your God is giving you rest and has given you this land.'

14. Your wives, your little ones, and your livestock shall remain in the land that Moses gave you on the other side of the Jordan. But you shall cross over before your brothers, armed for battle, all the mighty men of valor, and you shall help them,

15. until YHWH gives rest to your brothers as to you, and they also possess the land that YHWH your God is giving to them. Then you shall return to the land of your possession and shall possess it — the land that Moses the servant of YHWH gave to you on the other side of the Jordan, toward the rising of the sun."

16. And they answered Joshua, saying: "All that you have commanded us we will do, and wherever you send us we will go.

17. Just as we obeyed Moses in all things, so we will obey you. Only may YHWH your God be with you, as he was with Moses.

18. Any man who rebels against your command and does not obey your words in all that you command him shall be put to death. Only be strong and courageous."

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **YHWH (יהוה):** Rendered consistently as "YHWH" throughout, preserving the Tetragrammaton as it appears in the Leningrad Codex rather than substituting "the LORD" or "Adonai." This is a core LOGOS Bible convention.

2. **"servant of YHWH" (עֶבֶד יהוה):** The Hebrew *eved* can mean "servant" or "slave." "Servant" is chosen for Moses' title as it conveys covenantal service and honor rather than subjugation. The term is applied to Moses five times in this chapter (vv. 1, 2, 13, 15 [twice]), establishing Joshua's continuity with Mosaic authority.

3. **"minister" (מְשָׁרֵת, mesharet):** Distinguished from *eved* ("servant"). The term *mesharet* in v. 1 denotes an attendant or aide who serves in a more intimate, liturgical capacity — hence "minister" rather than "servant" to preserve the Hebrew distinction between Moses' role before YHWH (*eved*) and Joshua's role before Moses (*mesharet*).

4. **"Sons of Israel" (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל):** Rendered literally per LOGOS convention rather than the interpretive "Israelites."

5. **"Torah" (תּוֹרָה):** Retained in vv. 7–8 rather than translating as "law" or "instruction." The Hebrew *torah* in this context refers to the specific body of Mosaic teaching and carries more weight than the English word "law" conveys. Cross-reference with the LXX Vaticanus, which reads *nomos* (νόμος), confirms the reference is to the Mosaic legislation as a defined corpus.

6. **"act wisely" (תַּשְׂכִּיל, taskil):** The Hiphil of *sakal* in vv. 7–8 means "to be prudent, to have insight, to prosper through wisdom." Rendered "act wisely" rather than merely "have success" or "prosper" — the Hebrew conveys that success comes *through* wisdom and discernment, not as bare fortune. In v. 8b, both *tatsliach* ("make prosperous") and *taskil* ("act wisely") appear, and I have preserved the distinction.

7. **"Do not be terrified and do not be dismayed" (v. 9):** Hebrew *al-ta'arotz v'al-techat*. Two distinct terms for fear are preserved: *'arats* (to tremble/be terrified) and *chatat* (to be shattered/dismayed). The LXX Vaticanus similarly uses two terms (μὴ δειλιάσῃς μηδὲ φοβηθῇς), confirming the double exhortation.

8. **"armed for battle" (חֲמֻשִׁים, chamushim):** In v. 14, this term is debated — it may mean "in battle array" or "armed." The Vaticanus reads εὔζωνοι ("well-girded/lightly armed"). "Armed for battle" captures the military readiness the context demands.

9. **"toward the rising of the sun" / "toward the setting of the sun":** Literal rendering of *mizrach hashemesh* (v. 15) and *mevo hashemesh* (v. 4), preserving the Hebrew geographical idiom rather than substituting "east" and "west."

10. **Vaticanus cross-reference:** The LXX text on the Vaticanus folio was consulted throughout for confirmation of readings. No significant divergences from the Masoretic text of Joshua 1 were noted in this chapter; the Greek follows the Hebrew closely in both structure and content.

— LOGOS
